perturbation.original_text: Petro-Lube certifies that, in exercising its rights and carrying out its obligations under this Agreement, it shall comply with all applicable governmental laws, regulations, decrees and orders governing the export and re-export of goods, technology and software including, without limitation, the U.S. Export Administration Regulations and European Council Regulation 428/2009 and all amendments to such Regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Petro-Lube will endeavor to comply with governmental laws, regulations, decrees and orders governing the export and re-export of goods, technology and software, taking into account their understanding of U.S. Export Administration Regulations and European Council Regulation 428/2009 and amendments.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall comply' with 'will endeavor to comply' creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation. Adding 'taking into account their understanding' makes compliance subjective. This weakens the commitment to adhering to export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR requires strict compliance with export regulations. The modified text's ambiguous language ('endeavor to comply,' 'taking into account their understanding') contradicts this strict compliance requirement by allowing for subjective interpretation and potential non-compliance based on Petro-Lube's understanding, rather than adherence to the precise regulations.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with export regulations using "shall comply," while the changed text uses "will endeavor to comply," which weakens the obligation. The addition of "taking into account their understanding" introduces subjective interpretation, further diluting the compliance requirement. The Law Explanation highlights that the EAR requires strict compliance, which is contradicted by the modified text's ambiguous language. Although the scraped snippets don't explicitly state the phrase "strict compliance," Snippet 2 indicates that the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774 contains official rules, and Snippet 1 points to the eCFR, suggesting a detailed framework for compliance. This implies that the original 'shall comply' aligns more closely with the intended rigor of the EAR than the modified text, which allows for subjective interpretation and potential non-compliance. The change creates ambiguity, making the legal obligation difficult to enforce and contradicting the spirit of export regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Petro-Lube agrees and acknowledges that, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, (Your Company Name) makes no representation or warranty as to the satisfactory quality, suitability or fitness for purpose of any Samples and, save as expressly provided otherwise in this Agreement, shall not be liable, in negligence or otherwise, for any damage arising from or in connection with any use made by Petro- Lube of any Samples or any allegation that such use infringes any third party intellectual property rights or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Petro-Lube understands that (Your Company Name) provides Samples without guarantees of satisfactory quality, suitability, or fitness, and (Your Company Name) is not generally liable for damages arising from Sample use or IP infringements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly disclaims warranties to the fullest extent permitted by law, offering clear protection. The modified text uses softer language ('understands,' 'not generally liable'), creating ambiguity about the extent of liability disclaimer. It also removes the 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' clause, which could weaken the protection offered by the disclaimer.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 outlines specific requirements for disclaiming implied warranties. The modified text's use of vague language ('understands', 'not generally liable') and removal of the 'to the fullest extent permitted by law' clause may not meet the UCC's requirements for conspicuous and unequivocal disclaimer, potentially rendering the disclaimer unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the disclaimer of warranties, creating ambiguity and potentially contradicting UCC § 2-316. The original text used strong language ('to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law'), while the changed text uses vague language ('understands,' 'not generally liable'), which may not meet the UCC's requirement for a clear and conspicuous disclaimer as outlined in snippet 1 ('to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous').

perturbation.original_text: The obligations under this agreement shall continue to remain in force for a period of five (5) years from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties intend for the obligations under this agreement to remain in force for approximately five (5) years from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall continue to remain in force' to 'intend for the obligations to remain' introduces ambiguity about the enforceability of the obligations. Replacing 'five (5) years' with 'approximately five (5) years' further weakens the defined duration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations laws (Varies by State)
perturbation.law_explanation: Statutes of Limitations set specific time limits for legal actions. By using 'approximately five (5) years,' the agreement introduces uncertainty, making it difficult to determine the precise duration of the obligations and potentially conflicting with the state's statute of limitations for contract claims (New Jersey's is 6 years for contract claims under N.J. Stat. § 2A:14-1).

Every action at law for trespass to real property, for any tortious injury to real or personal property, for taking, detaining, or converting personal property, for replevin of goods or chattels, for any tortious injury to the rights of another not stated in N.J.S.2A:14-2 and N.J.S.2A:14-3, or for recovery upon a contractual claim or liability, express or implied, not under seal, or upon an account other than one which concerns the trade or merchandise between merchant and merchant, their factors, agents and servants, shall be commenced within six years next after the cause of any such action shall have accrued. b. This section shall not apply to any action for breach of any contract for sale governed by N.J.S.12A:2-725. c. The period of time for the filing of a claim by a condominium association, cooperative corporation, or other planned real estate development association against a developer or any person acting through, on behalf of or at the behest of the developer under subsection a. of this section, shall be tolled until an election is held and the owners comprise a majority of the board pursuant to paragraph (3) of subsection a. of section 5 of P.L.1993, c.30 (C.45:22A-47), or subsection d. of section 2 of P.L.1979, c.157 (C.46:8B-12.1). Any cause of action involving a condominium, cooperative, or other planned real estate development un
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates ambiguity regarding the duration of the agreement, potentially contradicting the statute of limitations. The original text specifies 'five (5) years,' whereas the changed text uses 'approximately five (5) years,' making the duration uncertain. According to N.J. Rev. Stat. § 2A:14-1, there is a 6-year statute of limitations for contract claims. The ambiguity introduced could lead to disputes regarding whether a claim is filed within the statutory period, thus creating a contradiction. The 'approximately' term weakens the enforceability by not providing a definitive time frame.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, and the relationship between (Your Company Name) and Petro- Lubricant Testing Laboratories, Inc. under this Agreement, shall in all respects be interpreted in accordance with and governed by the laws of the United States of America and the State of (Your State), and the Parties agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of those courts in relation to any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (whether based in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will generally be interpreted under the laws of the United States and the State of (Your State), and the Parties will consider submitting to the jurisdiction of those courts for disputes related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall in all respects be interpreted' to 'will generally be interpreted' and 'agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction' to 'will consider submitting to the jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity and weakens the governing law and jurisdiction clauses. The original clause clearly establishes the applicable law and the forum for dispute resolution, offering legal certainty. The modified language makes both aspects discretionary and uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 12(b)(2) allows a party to challenge the court's personal jurisdiction. A clear forum selection clause strengthens a court's jurisdiction. By making submission to the jurisdiction discretionary ('will consider submitting'), the modified text weakens the enforceability of the forum selection clause and could allow a party to more easily challenge personal jurisdiction, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to increased litigation costs.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing (a) Time to Serve a Responsive Pleading. Unless another time is specified by a federal statute, the time for serving a responsive pleading is as follows: (1) In General. (A) A defendant must serve an answer: (i) within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint; or (ii) if it has timely waived service under Rule 4(d) , within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. (B) A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim or crossclaim within 21 days after being served with the pleading that states the counterclaim or crossclaim. (C) A party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply, unless the order specifies a different time. (2) United States and Its Agencies, Officers, or Employees Sued in an Official Capacity. The United States, a United States agency, or a United States officer or employee sued only in an official capacity must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the United States attorney. (3) United States Officers or Employees Sued in an Individual Capacity. A United States officer or employee sued in an individual capacity for an act or omission occurring in connection with duties performed on the United States’ behalf must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the officer or employee or service on the United States attorney, whichever is later. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the enforceability of the forum selection clause. By changing 'shall in all respects be interpreted' to 'will generally be interpreted' and 'agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction' to 'will consider submitting to the jurisdiction,' the contract introduces ambiguity and makes both the applicable law and forum for dispute resolution discretionary. This directly contradicts the purpose of establishing clear jurisdiction and governing law, potentially leading to challenges in enforcing the agreement, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure's purpose is 'to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding.' By weakening the forum selection clause, the changed text undermines this purpose, potentially increasing litigation costs and delaying resolution.
